h1Title: Allergic Rhinitis (Hay Fever): Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Allergic rhinitis — commonly called hay fever — is an IgE-mediated inflammatory response of the nasal mucosa to airborne allergens including pollen, house dust mite, pet dander, and mould spores. It is one of the most prevalent chronic conditions worldwide, affecting 10-30% of adults and up to 40% of children (WHO ARIA 2008 guidelines). In the United States, approximately 26% of adults report a physician diagnosis of allergic rhinitis (CDC 2021). Symptoms include sneezing, nasal congestion, watery rhinorrhoea, and itchy eyes, which can significantly impair sleep, school performance, and work productivity. The majority of cases are controllable with a combination of allergen avoidance, second-generation antihistamines, and intranasal corticosteroids. Allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots or sublingual tablets) is the only disease-modifying treatment, capable of inducing long-term tolerance.

keyStats.prevalence: 10-30% of adults and 40% of children worldwide affected (WHO ARIA 2008); 26% of US adults report a physician diagnosis (CDC 2021)
keyStats.demographics: Onset most commonly in childhood or early adulthood (peak 10-20 years); atopic individuals (personal or family history of asthma, eczema, or food allergy) at highest risk
keyStats.avgAge: First symptoms most often appear age 5-20; prevalence peaks in teens and young adults; can occur at any age including first presentation in adulthood
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 400 million people affected globally (ARIA 2008); estimated societal cost in the US exceeds $11 billion annually including lost productivity

definition: Allergic rhinitis (ICD-10: J30) is a chronic IgE-mediated allergic inflammation of the nasal mucosa, classified by the ARIA (Allergic Rhinitis and its Impact on Asthma) guideline as intermittent (symptoms fewer than 4 days per week or fewer than 4 weeks per year) or persistent (symptoms more than 4 days per week AND more than 4 consecutive weeks). A severity dimension classifies disease as mild (no sleep impairment, no effect on daily activities) or moderate-severe (sleep disturbance, impaired activities, troublesome symptoms). Sensitisation occurs when allergen-specific IgE antibodies are produced by B cells after initial allergen exposure and bind to high-affinity IgE receptors on mast cells and basophils in the nasal mucosa. On re-exposure, allergen cross-links IgE, triggering mast-cell degranulation within seconds — the early-phase reaction — releasing histamine, leukotrienes, and prostaglandins that produce sneezing, rhinorrhoea, and nasal itch. A late-phase reaction 4-8 hours later involves cytokine-driven eosinophil and T-helper-2 lymphocyte infiltration, sustaining mucosal oedema and congestion. Allergic rhinitis is managed by allergists and immunologists, and frequently coexists with asthma (in up to 40% of cases), allergic conjunctivitis, sinusitis, and eczema, necessitating integrated atopic disease management.

typesClassification.description: Triggered by outdoor aeroallergens with defined seasons: tree pollen (February-May in temperate climates), grass pollen (May-July), and weed pollen (August-October). Symptoms are time-limited but can be severe during peak pollen counts above 50 grains/m³.

Triggered by indoor allergens present throughout the year: house dust mite (Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus and farinae), cat and dog dander, cockroach, and mould. Symptoms are persistent, often presenting as continuous nasal congestion and post-nasal drip.

Triggered by workplace aeroallergens including wheat flour (baker's asthma), latex, laboratory animal proteins, and wood dust. Symptoms improve on days away from work and worsen on return. Can progress to occupational asthma if the sensitiser exposure continues.

Localised IgE production in the nasal mucosa without detectable systemic sensitisation (negative skin-prick test and serum IgE). Diagnosed by nasal allergen provocation test. Accounts for 25-50% of rhinitis previously misclassified as non-allergic.

primarySymptoms: Repeated sneezing — often in clusters of 4-10 sneezes in rapid succession — occurring especially on waking or following allergen exposure such as entering a room with a cat.
Watery, clear nasal discharge (rhinorrhoea) that drips anteriorly from the nose or posteriorly into the throat (post-nasal drip), causing throat clearing and cough.
Nasal congestion and blockage from mucosal oedema, often bilateral and most severe in perennial disease, leading to mouth-breathing and sleep disruption.
Itching of the nose, soft palate, eyes, and ears — a characteristic atopic symptom that distinguishes allergic from infectious rhinitis.
Bilateral red, watery, itchy eyes (allergic conjunctivitis) — present in over 70% of seasonal allergic rhinitis patients and caused by conjunctival mast-cell activation.
Reduced or absent sense of smell (hyposmia or anosmia) from mucosal oedema obstructing the olfactory cleft.
Impaired sleep quality from nocturnal nasal congestion, post-nasal drip-related cough, and mouth-breathing, resulting in daytime fatigue and reduced cognitive performance.
Dark circles under the eyes (allergic shiners) from venous pooling secondary to chronic nasal congestion.
Allergic salute — a transverse nasal crease across the nose bridge from repeated upward rubbing to relieve itch, particularly visible in children.
earlyWarningSigns: Recurrent sneezing, runny nose, and nasal itch that appear annually at the same season in a child or young adult — classic presentation of seasonal sensitisation.
Persistent nocturnal nasal congestion with poor sleep in a child, often attributed to colds but actually caused by house dust mite sensitisation.
Recurrent bilateral red itchy eyes without discharge that responds to antihistamines — suggests allergic conjunctivitis accompanying rhinitis.
Frequent sneezing in specific environments (visiting a friend with a cat, dusty rooms, freshly cut grass) — exposure history guides allergen testing.
emergencySigns: Severe acute urticaria, throat swelling, wheezing, or anaphylaxis following a new allergen exposure or immunotherapy injection — administer adrenaline and call emergency services immediately.
New-onset severe wheezing or breathlessness in a patient with hay fever — may represent the first episode of allergen-triggered asthma; requires immediate bronchodilator and urgent assessment.

High fever with unilateral facial pain, toothache-like pain, and purulent nasal discharge lasting more than 10 days — suggests acute bacterial sinusitis as a complication requiring antibiotic evaluation.
Sudden complete unilateral nasal obstruction with facial pain or blood-stained discharge — warrants ENT evaluation to exclude polyp, deviated septum, or neoplasm.

causes.cause: Airborne pollen from trees, grasses, and weeds
causes.description: Seasonal allergic rhinitis is driven by inhalation of pollen grains 10-100 µm in diameter during peak pollen season. Major grass pollen allergens (Phl p 1 and Phl p 5 from Timothy grass) are the most prevalent sensitisers in temperate Europe and North America, affecting 40-60% of allergic rhinitis patients. Climate change is extending pollen seasons and increasing pollen load globally.

Dermatophagoides pteronyssinus and D. farinae are microscopic arachnids that live in bedding, carpets, and soft furnishings, producing proteolytic allergens (Der p 1, Der f 1) in their faeces. HDM is the dominant perennial allergen in humid climates, sensitising approximately 20% of the general population and 50-80% of atopic individuals. Mite density above 2 µg Der p 1/g of dust is associated with sensitisation.

Cat allergen Fel d 1 (produced in sebaceous glands and saliva) is particularly persistent: it remains airborne for hours on particles as small as 2-5 µm and is detectable in cat-free buildings for months after a cat has been removed. Dog allergen Can f 1 is similarly sticky. Cat sensitisation affects 10-20% of adults in developed countries.

Outdoor mould levels peak in late summer and autumn; indoor mould thrives in damp, poorly ventilated environments. Alternaria alternata sensitisation is strongly associated with severe asthma in children. Spore counts above 1,000 spores/m³ correlate with increased emergency asthma presentations.

causes.cause: Cockroach and rodent allergens in urban environments
causes.description: Cockroach allergens Bla g 1 and Bla g 2 are major sensitisers in inner-city populations, particularly in North America. Inner-city children sensitised to cockroach allergen have 2-3× higher asthma morbidity than non-sensitised children (NCICAS data). Rodent urinary proteins (Mus m 1) sensitise laboratory workers and increasingly urban residents.

riskFactors.factor: Personal or family history of atopy
riskFactors.description: Atopic individuals — those with personal or family history of asthma, eczema, or food allergy — have a 3-5× higher risk of developing allergic rhinitis. Having one allergic parent confers 30-40% risk; two allergic parents raises it to 60-70%. Multiple susceptibility loci including 17q21 and IL-13/IL-4 pathway variants are implicated.

The hygiene hypothesis and biodiversity hypothesis propose that reduced microbial diversity in early life — from urban living, antibiotic use, reduced sibship, and caesarean delivery — impairs regulatory T-cell development and increases atopic sensitisation risk. Children on farms exposed to diverse microbes have substantially lower allergy rates.

Diesel exhaust particles potentiate IgE responses to allergens by acting as adjuvants. NO2 and particulate matter above 2.5 µm damage epithelial tight junctions, facilitating allergen penetration. Urban children have higher allergic rhinitis prevalence than rural peers even after adjustment for pollen exposure.

Both active smoking and early-life passive smoke exposure increase IgE sensitisation risk. Parental smoking in the first year of life is associated with 30-50% higher allergic rhinitis risk in the child.

riskFactors.factor: Male sex in childhood, female predominance in adulthood
riskFactors.description: Boys have higher atopy rates than girls before puberty; the sex ratio reverses in adulthood, with women more commonly developing new-onset allergic rhinitis. Hormonal influences on IgE regulation are postulated.

Warmer climates produce longer pollen seasons and higher cumulative pollen loads. Climate change has increased northern European pollen season by 10-20 days and pollen concentrations by 10-30% since the 1970s.

affectedDemographics: Children and teenagers: prevalence peaks at 10-20 years; approximately 40% of children with eczema will develop allergic rhinitis (atopic march).
Young adults aged 20-40 represent the highest economic burden group due to work and study impairment; workplace productivity loss averages 1-5 hours per week during peak season.
Inner-city children: substantially higher rates of perennial allergic rhinitis from cockroach and dust mite sensitisation, associated with worse asthma outcomes.
Healthcare workers and laboratory staff: occupational sensitisation to latex, animal dander, and pharmaceutical allergens represent a distinct high-risk occupational group.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of allergic rhinitis follows the ARIA 2020 and BSACI guidelines. A structured clinical history is the foundation: characterise symptom onset (seasonal vs perennial), triggers (specific exposures that worsen symptoms), presence of ocular symptoms, and family or personal atopic history. Physical examination assesses the nasal mucosa (bluish-pink, boggy, oedematous turbinates are characteristic), allergic salute crease, and conjunctival injection. Allergy testing confirms sensitisation and identifies the causative allergen(s). Skin-prick testing (SPT) is the preferred first-line test in most guidelines: a drop of standardised allergen extract placed on the forearm is pricked through with a lancet; a wheal of 3 mm or more above the negative control at 15 minutes is positive. SPT is fast (results in 15-20 minutes), inexpensive, and highly sensitive (80-90% for common inhalant allergens). Serum specific IgE testing (ImmunoCAP) provides equivalent information and is preferred when SPT is impractical — in patients on antihistamines, with severe eczema, or with high risk of systemic reaction. Component-resolved diagnostics (CRD) identifies which molecular allergen component is responsible, distinguishing genuine sensitisation from cross-reactive carbohydrate determinants and guiding immunotherapy. Nasal provocation test is the gold standard for diagnosing local allergic rhinitis and equivocal cases. Rhinoscopy and sinus CT are reserved for suspected nasal polyps, structural abnormality, or chronic rhinosinusitis complicating the picture.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line allergy test to confirm IgE-mediated sensitisation to specific inhalant allergens (pollen, HDM, pet dander, mould)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A drop of allergen extract is placed on the forearm and a small prick is made through the drop. 15-20 minutes later the nurse measures any wheal (raised lump). A positive result is a wheal 3 mm larger than the negative control. Mild itch at the test site is common; systemic reactions are very rare. Antihistamines must be stopped 3-7 days beforehand.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures blood levels of IgE antibodies to specific allergens; alternative to SPT when skin testing is impractical
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results in 3-5 days. Quantitative results in kU/L; class 2 (0.35-0.7 kU/L) and above are clinically relevant. Does not require antihistamine withdrawal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the specific molecular allergen component causing sensitisation; distinguishes true sensitisation from cross-reactivity; essential before prescribing immunotherapy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw, same as specific IgE. Results in 5-7 days. Example: positive Phl p 1 and Phl p 5 (genuine grass sensitisation) vs. positive only to cross-reactive components (less likely to respond to immunotherapy).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold standard for confirming allergen-specific nasal reactivity; used to diagnose local allergic rhinitis when SPT and specific IgE are negative despite a typical clinical history
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A measured dose of allergen solution is instilled into one nostril using a spray; symptoms (sneezing, rhinorrhoea) and nasal airflow resistance are assessed at 15 minutes and at 4-8 hours. Takes 30-60 minutes and is performed in a specialist centre.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Visualises nasal polyps, deviated septum, turbinate hypertrophy, and purulent secretions; excludes structural diagnoses mimicking or complicating rhinitis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin flexible camera inserted through one nostril after topical decongestant spray. Takes 5-10 minutes and is mildly uncomfortable. No sedation required. Performed by ENT or specialist allergist.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows the ARIA 2020 stepwise algorithm, adjusted for intermittent vs persistent and mild vs moderate-severe classification. For mild intermittent disease, oral second-generation antihistamines (loratadine, cetirizine, fexofenadine) or intranasal antihistamines (azelastine) used on demand are first-line. Second-generation antihistamines are preferred over first-generation agents (chlorphenamine, promethazine) because they are non-sedating and have a longer duration. For persistent or moderate-severe disease, intranasal corticosteroids (INCS) — fluticasone propionate, mometasone furoate, budesonide — are the most effective single agent in guidelines, reducing all four nasal symptom domains and ocular symptoms through their broad anti-inflammatory effect; they are more effective than oral antihistamines in head-to-head trials. INCS require 1-3 days to reach onset and 2 weeks for maximum effect; patients should be counselled to use them regularly and not just on demand. Fixed-dose intranasal combination formulations (azelastine plus fluticasone in a single device — Dymista) provide faster relief than INCS alone. Leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast) are a third-line option when INCS or antihistamines are insufficient, but NICE downgraded montelukast to third-line in 2021 due to neuropsychiatric risk signals (depression, anxiety, suicidal ideation) requiring prescriber-patient discussion. For moderate-severe perennial or poly-sensitised seasonal disease, allergen immunotherapy (AIT) is the only treatment that modifies the underlying immune response and produces long-lasting tolerance after discontinuation. Subcutaneous immunotherapy (SCIT) runs 3-5 years; sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT) tablets or drops are administered at home. Both are Class I evidence in ARIA and reduce symptoms by 30-40% compared with pharmacotherapy alone.

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal corticosteroids (INCS) — fluticasone propionate 50 µg/spray, mometasone 50 µg/spray, budesonide 100 µg/spray
medicalTreatments.description: Gold-standard first-line treatment for persistent or moderate-severe allergic rhinitis. Delivered directly to the nasal mucosa with minimal systemic absorption, reducing mucosal oedema, eosinophil infiltration, and mast-cell activation. Must be used daily for 2-4 weeks for full effect. Aqueous formulations are preferred over aerosol for patient adherence and reduced epistaxis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses: INCS reduces total nasal symptom score by 30-40% vs. placebo; superior to oral antihistamines for congestion (OR 1.8); reduces ocular symptoms in 70% of patients with concurrent allergic conjunctivitis (PMID 17374815)

medicalTreatments.name: Second-generation oral antihistamines — cetirizine 10 mg/day, loratadine 10 mg/day, fexofenadine 120-180 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Block H1 histamine receptors on nasal mucosa and conjunctivae, rapidly relieving sneezing, rhinorrhoea, and itch within 1 hour. Non-sedating or minimally sedating at standard doses. Effective for intermittent disease and as add-on to INCS for moderate-severe seasonal disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce symptom scores by 20-30% vs. placebo; faster onset (1-3 hours) than INCS; less effective for congestion; cetirizine has slightly higher sedation rate than loratadine or fexofenadine

medicalTreatments.name: Intranasal azelastine 0.1% nasal spray (1-2 sprays per nostril twice daily) or combined azelastine/fluticasone
medicalTreatments.description: Intranasal antihistamine with anti-inflammatory properties; faster onset than INCS (within 15-30 minutes). The combination product (azelastine 137 µg + fluticasone 50 µg per actuation, Dymista) is more effective than either component alone and is particularly suitable for moderate-severe disease requiring rapid relief alongside daily anti-inflammatory cover.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PNSS reduction of 30-35% vs. placebo; combination product superior to fluticasone alone by 10-15% in head-to-head trials (PMID 23062248)

medicalTreatments.name: Leukotriene receptor antagonist — montelukast 10 mg/day
medicalTreatments.description: Third-line adjunct when INCS plus antihistamine are insufficient, or in patients with concurrent asthma where a single agent can address both conditions. Note: MHRA and FDA issued neuropsychiatric warnings in 2019-2020; prescribers must discuss risks of depression, anxiety, sleep disorders, and suicidal ideation with patients and carers before prescribing.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces nasal symptom score by 15-20% vs. placebo; less effective than INCS but useful as add-on. Cochrane 2014: montelukast broadly equivalent to antihistamine alone (PMID 25101727)

medicalTreatments.name: Allergen immunotherapy (SCIT or SLIT)
medicalTreatments.description: The only disease-modifying treatment. Subcutaneous (SCIT) involves escalating doses of allergen injected weekly for 3-6 months (build-up phase), then monthly for 3-5 years (maintenance). Sublingual tablets (SLIT, e.g. grass pollen SLIT-tablet Grazax, HDM Acarizax) are taken daily at home. Both modalities induce regulatory T-cells and Treg-mediated immune tolerance, reducing symptoms and medication use during and for years after completion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cochrane 2010 (SCIT, PMID 16034940): significant improvement in symptom and medication scores. Meta-analyses of grass SLIT: symptom score reduction 30-40% vs. placebo with effect lasting ≥3 years post-treatment. Also reduces risk of new sensitisations and asthma development.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nasal saline irrigation (large-volume low-pressure or neti pot)
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily saline rinse (isotonic or hypertonic) mechanically removes allergens and crusts from the nasal mucosa, reduces mucosal oedema, and improves mucociliary function. RCT and Cochrane data support an adjunct role alongside INCS, reducing symptom scores and the need for rescue medication. Safe for all ages.

alternativeTreatments.name: Allergen avoidance measures for house dust mite
alternativeTreatments.description: Encasing mattresses, pillows, and duvets in allergen-impermeable covers, washing bedding at 60°C weekly, and removing carpets reduces Der p 1 exposure by 50-80%. Cochrane reviews show modest but consistent symptom benefit when HDM avoidance is combined with medical therapy (PMID 18254000).

whySeeSpecialist: An allergist or immunologist should be consulted when symptoms are severe, perennial, not controlled by 2-step pharmacotherapy, suspected to be driven by multiple allergens, or when allergen immunotherapy is being considered. Specialist input is also needed when distinguishing from non-allergic rhinitis, when rhinitis is complicated by sinusitis or polyps, or when concurrent asthma is present and a unified atopic management plan is needed.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a board-certified allergist with expertise in inhalant allergy testing and allergen immunotherapy. Centres offering component-resolved diagnostics and in-house skin-prick testing provide the most accurate sensitisation profiles. For children, a paediatric allergist with experience in the atopic march (eczema-rhinitis-asthma sequence) is preferred.
hospitalCriteria: Allergen challenge suite for nasal provocation tests
In-house allergen extract library for skin-prick testing across major inhalant panels
Allergen immunotherapy preparation and monitoring capability
Paediatric allergy clinic for children with multi-system atopic disease
ENT collaboration for patients with nasal polyps or structural rhinitis
keyFacilities: Accredited allergy and immunology outpatient clinics
Combined allergy-asthma clinics for unified atopic management
preventionStrategies: Reduce house dust mite exposure with allergen-impermeable mattress and pillow covers, weekly bedding washes at 60°C, and reduced carpet use — most effective for HDM-sensitised individuals.
Avoid exposing atopic infants to tobacco smoke — both prenatal and postnatal passive smoke exposure significantly increase allergic sensitisation risk.
Monitor local pollen forecasts during peak season (mobile apps such as Pollen.com or Met Office pollen level alerts) and stay indoors or limit outdoor activity when counts are above 50 grains/m³.
Wear wraparound sunglasses outdoors during pollen season to reduce conjunctival allergen exposure.
Consider starting allergen immunotherapy at the first sign of polysensitisation or moderate-severe disease to prevent progression to asthma — the atopic march.
lifestyleModifications: Use intranasal corticosteroid spray consistently every day during pollen season (starting 2 weeks before season onset for maximum efficacy) and year-round for perennial disease.
Shower and change clothes after outdoor activities during peak pollen days to remove pollen from hair and skin.
Keep windows closed at home and in the car during peak pollen times; use air conditioning with HEPA filtration where available.
Vacuum carpets and soft furnishings weekly with a HEPA-filtered vacuum cleaner; damp-dust hard surfaces to remove settled allergens.
Avoid pets in the bedroom; if a cat or dog is present, run a HEPA air purifier in the bedroom and keep the animal out of sleeping areas.
Take prescribed antihistamine or INCS before exposure to known allergen triggers (attending a friend with a cat, visiting a garden) to reduce the peak early-phase response.

recommended: Omega-3-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for anti-inflammatory properties — observational data associate higher omega-3 intake with lower atopic sensitisation in children

Probiotic-containing foods (live yoghurt, kefir) — some evidence for reduced allergic rhinitis severity with regular probiotic intake, particularly in pollen season (Cochrane 2015 moderate quality evidence)

Vitamin D-rich foods (oily fish, eggs, fortified dairy) — vitamin D deficiency is associated with higher allergic rhinitis severity; supplementation to normal levels (50-125 nmol/L) is reasonable in deficient patients
Plenty of fresh fruit and vegetables for antioxidant polyphenols (quercetin, resveratrol) that may modulate mast-cell degranulation
avoid: Oral allergy syndrome triggers for pollen-sensitised patients: raw apples, hazelnuts, peaches, and carrots cross-react with birch pollen Bet v 1 and can cause oropharyngeal itch; cooking destroys the allergen
Alcohol during pollen season — ethanol increases nasal blood flow and worsens mucosal oedema; wine and beer also contain histamine and sulphites that can exacerbate symptoms
Spicy foods in patients with marked nasal hyperreactivity — capsaicin can trigger non-allergic rhinitis symptoms superimposed on underlying allergic disease
Known food cross-reactors for sensitised patients confirmed by SPT or CRD before elimination

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate-intensity aerobic exercise has anti-inflammatory effects and improves overall quality of life in allergic rhinitis, and is recommended. During outdoor pollen season, exercise indoors or early morning before peak midday-afternoon pollen release. Swimming is particularly well-tolerated. If exercise consistently triggers severe rhinitis or wheezing, evaluation for exercise-induced bronchoconstriction is warranted. Antihistamine pre-treatment or pre-exercise nasal saline may reduce exercise-triggered rhinitis flares.
dailyManagement: Use intranasal corticosteroid spray every morning (both nostrils; tilt head slightly forward, aim toward the lateral wall, not the septum) to prevent epistaxis.
Take second-generation antihistamine at the same time daily during season, or as needed for breakthrough symptoms; avoid first-generation agents if driving or operating machinery.
Check the pollen forecast each morning during season; plan high-exposure activities accordingly.
Carry a rescue antihistamine (cetirizine or fexofenadine) for unexpected high-exposure situations.
Attend allergen immunotherapy injection appointments on schedule — missing injections may require dose reduction; notify the clinic if you are unwell.
Report any new-onset wheeze, chest tightness, or breathlessness to your allergist promptly — these signal possible progression to allergic asthma.
prognosis: Allergic rhinitis is a lifelong condition for most patients, but its natural history is variable. Approximately 50-60% of patients have persistent symptoms into adulthood; 20-30% experience spontaneous improvement over decades, and 10-15% achieve clinical remission, particularly after reduced allergen exposure. Seasonal grass pollen rhinitis tends to remit more often than perennial HDM disease. The main risk of under-treated allergic rhinitis is the atopic march: 30-40% of patients with allergic rhinitis develop asthma if untreated, particularly those sensitised to HDM, Alternaria, or multiple allergens simultaneously. Allergen immunotherapy reduces this risk by approximately 50% over 3 years (preventive AIT trials). Quality of life is significantly impaired during symptomatic periods — performance decrements of 20-40% on cognitive testing have been documented during peak pollen season in untreated students. With guideline-adherent pharmacotherapy and immunotherapy where indicated, most patients achieve excellent symptom control.
recoveryTimeline: Intranasal corticosteroids reach partial effect in 3-7 days and maximum effect in 2-4 weeks; antihistamines provide relief within 1-3 hours. Allergen immunotherapy: meaningful symptom reduction typically appears after 4-6 months of build-up and the first maintenance year; full benefit requires 3-5 years of treatment. Maintained tolerance after discontinuation of SCIT or SLIT lasts 3 or more years for most patients. Seasonal disease resolves between seasons but returns each spring or autumn without preventive treatment.
complications: Asthma development — 30-40% of patients with moderate-severe untreated allergic rhinitis develop asthma; the two conditions share the same mucosal inflammatory pathway and should be managed as a unified airway disease.
Chronic rhinosinusitis and nasal polyps — untreated perennial allergic rhinitis drives mucosal oedema that obstructs sinus ostia, promoting bacterial overgrowth and, over years, polyp formation.
Eustachian tube dysfunction and otitis media with effusion — mucosal oedema extends to the Eustachian tube, impairing drainage and causing conductive hearing loss, particularly in children.
Sleep-disordered breathing — nasal obstruction from allergic rhinitis contributes to snoring and worsens obstructive sleep apnoea, increasing daytime sleepiness and cardiovascular risk.

Educational and occupational impairment — objective testing demonstrates 20-40% cognitive performance reduction during untreated hay fever; accounts for estimated $4 billion annual productivity loss in the US.

Anaphylaxis risk during allergen immunotherapy — build-up phase carries a 1:1 million risk of systemic anaphylaxis per injection; all SCIT must be administered under medical supervision with 30-minute observation.

supportResources.name: Allergy UK — Hay Fever and Allergic Rhinitis
supportResources.description: UK charity providing patient information on pollen types, monitoring, allergen avoidance, and immunotherapy access.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Allergy Asthma & Immunology (AAAAI) — Rhinitis
supportResources.description: Evidence-based patient library on allergic rhinitis diagnosis, treatment options, and finding a board-certified allergist.

supportResources.name: Asthma + Lung UK — Hay Fever
supportResources.description: Guidance on managing hay fever as an asthma trigger, with pollen alerts and peak-season management plans.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes lower airway symptoms — wheeze, chest tightness, breathlessness, and nocturnal cough — from bronchospasm, not nasal mucosa inflammation. However, the two commonly coexist as unified airway disease; 40% of allergic rhinitis patients have asthma. Asthma is diagnosed by spirometry and reversibility testing; rhinitis by clinical examination and allergy testing.

confusedWithConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis (Eczema)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic dermatitis is a skin condition causing itchy, dry, inflamed skin, not nasal symptoms. Both are IgE-mediated atopic diseases that frequently coexist as part of the atopic march. A child with eczema has a 30-40% chance of developing allergic rhinitis; they share allergen triggers but have distinct clinical presentations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Urticaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic urticaria causes raised, itchy wheals and angioedema on the skin, primarily driven by spontaneous rather than allergen-triggered mast-cell activation. Most chronic urticaria is not IgE-mediated and allergy testing is negative; a positive skin-prick test in urticaria patients usually reflects coincidental atopy rather than the cause of wheals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Common Cold (Viral Rhinitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral rhinitis causes green or yellow mucoid discharge, sore throat, fever, and body aches, typically resolving within 7-10 days. Allergic rhinitis causes clear watery discharge, itching, sneezing clusters, and bilateral eye symptoms, persisting for weeks during allergen exposure without fever. Skin-prick testing distinguishes them definitively.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the same IgE-mediated airway inflammation; 40% of allergic rhinitis patients develop asthma; the two are now managed as unified airway disease per ARIA/GINA.

relatedConditions.name: Atopic Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Part of the atopic triad; childhood eczema typically precedes rhinitis by the atopic march, with the same Th2 immune pathway.

relatedConditions.name: Hives (Urticaria)
relatedConditions.relevance: Both involve mast-cell activation and histamine release; allergic rhinitis patients have higher rates of urticaria from cross-reactive food allergens.

relatedConditions.name: Psoriasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Psoriasis, though Th17-mediated, co-occurs with atopic disease at higher than expected rates and shares biological treatment targets including IL-4/IL-13 pathways in dupilumab-responsive phenotypes.

faqs.question: What is hay fever?
faqs.answer: Hay fever is seasonal allergic rhinitis — an allergic reaction to airborne pollen from trees, grasses, or weeds that inflames the nasal lining. It causes sneezing, runny nose, nasal congestion, and itchy, watery eyes. It affects 10-30% of adults worldwide and is managed with antihistamines, nasal steroid sprays, and allergen immunotherapy.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of allergic rhinitis?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include repeated sneezing, clear watery nasal discharge, nasal congestion and blockage, nasal and eye itching, and bilateral red watery eyes. Persistent disease adds reduced sense of smell, post-nasal drip, disturbed sleep, and fatigue. Unlike a cold, allergic rhinitis does not cause fever or thick coloured mucus.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hay fever and a cold?
faqs.answer: Hay fever has clear watery discharge, bilateral itchy eyes, sneezing in clusters, and no fever, persisting as long as allergen exposure continues. A cold causes thick coloured mucus, sore throat, mild fever, and body aches, and resolves within 7-10 days. Skin-prick allergy testing confirms hay fever.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for allergic rhinitis?
faqs.answer: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays (fluticasone, mometasone) are the most effective single treatment for persistent or moderate-severe symptoms, recommended by all major guidelines. They reduce sneezing, runny nose, congestion, and eye symptoms when used regularly. Non-sedating antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) are added for breakthrough symptoms.

faqs.question: Can allergic rhinitis cause asthma?
faqs.answer: Yes. 30-40% of people with moderate-severe allergic rhinitis develop asthma over time, a process called the atopic march. Both conditions share IgE-mediated airway inflammation. Treating allergic rhinitis with allergen immunotherapy reduces the risk of developing asthma by approximately 50% over 3 years.

faqs.question: What is allergen immunotherapy (allergy shots)?
faqs.answer: Allergen immunotherapy involves gradually increasing doses of the causative allergen — given as injections (SCIT) or sublingual tablets (SLIT) — to retrain the immune system to tolerate it. It is the only disease-modifying treatment for allergic rhinitis, reducing symptoms by 30-40% and providing benefit for 3 or more years after stopping treatment.

faqs.question: Is hay fever worse in the morning?
faqs.answer: Yes. Grass and tree pollen levels peak between 5 a.m. and 10 a.m. as temperatures rise and wind disperses pollen. Symptoms are typically most severe on waking. Checking local pollen forecasts and keeping bedroom windows closed overnight substantially reduces morning exposure and symptom severity.

faqs.question: Can children have allergen immunotherapy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Subcutaneous immunotherapy is approved from age 5 and sublingual grass pollen tablets from age 5-6 in most countries. Immunotherapy in children provides additional benefits beyond symptom control: it can prevent the development of new allergic sensitisations and reduce the risk of asthma emerging.

faqs.question: What foods trigger hay fever?
faqs.answer: Patients allergic to birch pollen often react to raw apples, hazelnuts, peaches, celery, and carrots due to cross-reactive proteins — a phenomenon called oral allergy syndrome. Symptoms are mild oropharyngeal itch. Cooking destroys the protein, making these foods safe cooked. Specific CRD testing confirms which allergen component is responsible.

faqs.question: How long does hay fever season last?
faqs.answer: In temperate climates, tree pollen peaks February to May, grass pollen May to July, and weed pollen August to October. Total pollen season may span March-October. Climate change has extended seasons by 10-20 days since the 1970s. Perennial rhinitis from dust mite or pet dander causes year-round symptoms.

faqs.question: Do antihistamines stop working over time?
faqs.answer: No. Tolerance to second-generation antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine) does not develop at standard doses. If symptoms seem less controlled, disease severity may have increased, pollen counts may be higher, or adherence may have lapsed. Switching to an intranasal corticosteroid or adding allergen immunotherapy rather than rotating antihistamines is the evidence-based step-up.

faqs.question: What is the difference between seasonal and perennial allergic rhinitis?
faqs.answer: Seasonal allergic rhinitis (hay fever) is triggered by outdoor pollen and causes symptoms during specific months each year. Perennial allergic rhinitis is year-round and triggered by indoor allergens — mainly house dust mites, cat, dog, or cockroach. Both are confirmed by skin-prick testing and treated with the same classes of medication.

faqs.question: Can allergic rhinitis cause headaches?
faqs.answer: Facial pressure and pain resembling sinus headache are common in allergic rhinitis from mucosal oedema obstructing sinus ostia. True migraines can also be triggered by histamine and other inflammatory mediators released during allergic reactions. If headaches are severe or persistent, sinus CT can exclude chronic rhinosinusitis as a complication.

faqs.question: Is allergic rhinitis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. One allergic parent confers 30-40% risk; two allergic parents raise the risk to 60-70%. Multiple genes in the IL-4/IL-13 and IgE regulatory pathway are implicated. Environmental factors (allergen exposure, pollution, diet, microbiome diversity) interact with genetic susceptibility to determine whether sensitisation occurs.

faqs.question: Can you grow out of hay fever?
faqs.answer: About 20-30% of patients experience spontaneous improvement in adulthood, particularly those with seasonal grass pollen rhinitis. Perennial HDM rhinitis is less likely to remit spontaneously. Allergen immunotherapy produces durable tolerance that lasts 3 or more years after stopping, and is the closest thing to a cure currently available.

metaTitle: Allergic Rhinitis: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Allergic rhinitis (hay fever): pollen or dust mite causes sneezing, runny nose, itchy eyes. Antihistamines, nasal steroids, and immunotherapy reviewed.
